text:
This chapter analyzes the new dynamics emerging between research and local integration policy in Europe. Although national frames of reference have traditionally defined migration studies, the intervention of cities in the design of their own policies of integration has become more and more manifest and this chapter examines in detail the new policy-research nexus which developed in the Netherlands and in Sweden over the last thirty years and analyzed in depth here: the intervention of research in the triple phase of problem definition, policy design and policy implementation.

This chapter will specifically look into how the new EU integration mechanisms set up since 2003 have come to shape multi-level governance. Indeed, the author shows that the recent years have seen an increase of “horizontal cooperation”, where cities participate in international networks (for example CLIP, Integrating Cities or the Intercultural Cities initiative) in order to circumvent the national level and carry out collaborative work on the best practices to solve problems such as housing, diverse labor markets or discrimination. All in all, the author finds that the activities of local research-policy dialogue that take place in the shadow of academic research and sometimes also in the shadows of policy debates – as a result of being relegated to the more obscure “grey literature” and in part due to linguistic obstacles – remain a highly important and practice-based aspect of the wider politics of migrant and refugee inclusion that continue to unfold at the municipal level through continuous and dynamic modes of collaboration between municipal authorities, research centres and European level actors.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
